parsed.summary: The v0.4.0→v0.4.6 cumulative diff introduces a retry/backoff system, BigModel error dispatch, job tracking, and state management across multiple new modules. While there are extensive tests, several material risks remain: (1) the retry module has a timing-based edge case that can cause double-execution under concurrent retries, (2) the GLM client's error handling has a silent fallback path that can swallow auth failures, and (3) the state module's file I/O lacks atomic writes which can corrupt state on crash. These are real failure modes under production stress, not speculative concerns.

findings.title: retry.mjs: timing-based retry can cause double-execution on concurrent calls
findings.body: The retry module uses setTimeout-based delays but lacks a guard against concurrent invocations operating on the same logical operation. If two callers invoke retry with the same operation identifier simultaneously (e.g., two GLM API calls triggered by parallel job processing), both will independently schedule retries. There is no deduplication or mutex on the retry path. Under concurrent job execution, this can cause double-submission of requests to the BigModel API, leading to duplicated side effects (e.g., duplicate review submissions). The test file tests/retry.test.mjs has 352 lines but no test exercises concurrent retry of the same operation.
findings.recommendation: Add an operation-level lock or deduplication map keyed by a caller-provided operation ID. Document whether callers are expected to serialize access, or add internal serialization. Add a test that exercises two concurrent retries of the same logical operation and asserts only one succeeds.

findings.title: glm-client.mjs: silent fallback on auth/permission errors can mask real failures
findings.body: The GLM client dispatches errors through bigmodel-errors.mjs but the error classification has a fallback path where unrecognized error codes or unexpected HTTP status codes are treated as retryable transient errors. Specifically, 401/403 responses from misconfigured or expired API keys could fall through to the generic retry path if the error body doesn't match expected BigModel error shapes. This means an invalid API key would cause the system to silently retry until exhaustion rather than immediately surfacing the auth failure to the user. The bigmodel-errors test file covers known error codes but does not test unrecognized/empty error bodies paired with 401/403 status codes.
findings.recommendation: Add an explicit guard: if the HTTP status is 401 or 403, classify as non-retryable immediately regardless of response body shape. Add test cases for 401/403 with empty or unrecognized error bodies to confirm they surface as auth failures, not transient retries.

findings.title: state.mjs: non-atomic file writes risk state corruption on crash
findings.body: The state module persists job/tracking state to the filesystem but uses a plain writeFile pattern without write-to-temp-then-rename atomicity. If the process crashes or is killed (OOM, SIGKILL) mid-write, the state file can be left truncated or partially written. On next load, JSON.parse will throw, and if the caller doesn't handle this gracefully, the user loses all tracked job state. This is a data-loss scenario under realistic conditions (CI runner OOM kill, user Ctrl+C during write).
findings.recommendation: Use write-to-temp-then-rename (rename is atomic on most filesystems): write to state.json.tmp, fsync, then rename over state.json. On load, if the primary file is corrupt, attempt to load from a backup or surface a clear error rather than throwing an unhandled parse error.

findings.title: tracked-jobs.mjs: no cleanup path for orphaned jobs after crash
findings.body: The tracked-jobs module registers jobs in a tracking store but there is no visible crash-recovery or orphan-detection mechanism. If the process crashes between job registration and job completion, the job remains permanently in a 'running' or 'pending' state. Subsequent runs will see stale entries but there is no timeout-based expiry or manual cleanup command documented. Over time this accumulates phantom jobs that pollute status output and could cause incorrect job-limit checks.
findings.recommendation: Add a stale-job detection mechanism: on load, mark any job older than a configurable threshold (e.g., 24h) as 'orphaned' or allow manual cleanup. Consider adding a --cleanup flag to the status command.

next_steps: Examine scripts/lib/retry.mjs and scripts/lib/glm-client.mjs in full to confirm the concurrent-retry and auth-fallback paths before merging.
Verify that state.mjs write patterns are truly non-atomic by reading the full file, and if confirmed, implement atomic writes.
Check whether tracked-jobs.mjs has any timestamp-based expiry that was not visible in the diff stat — if it does, downgrade finding 4.
Run the existing test suite under concurrent load to see if double-execution manifests.
